result.topicName: Event loop, call stack, microtasks vs macrotasks

answers.7:
async function foo() { console.log('1'); await Promise.resolve(); console.log('2'); } console.log('3'); foo(); console.log('4'); 

=====Execution starts===== 
Event loop picks the first line foo() gets initialised and memory references are created. 
Event loop picks the next line of code in order 
3 ---> Prints 
Event loop picks the next line of code in order 
foo() ---> function is called 
Event loop now run foo synchronously as there is no await before foo(); picks the next line of code 
1 --> Prints 
Event loop sees await and pushes the task in micro queue and picks the next line of code in order 
4 ---> Prints No more lines of code/tasks to do so the event loop is free. 
It checks Micro-task queue now. It sees a task and picks it 
2 --> Prints
answers.8: Reacts decision to use macro-task over micro-task is to ensure that the rendering is not starving the event loop. If this starts yielding on the micro-tasks then any priority tasks might get blocked. For instance a user starts typing which is an important task which needs to be given more importance. This prevent janky experience.
answers.9:
console.log('start'); setTimeout(() => { console.log('timeout 1'); Promise.resolve().then(() => console.log('promise inside timeout')); }, 0); Promise.resolve() .then(() => { console.log('promise 1'); setTimeout(() => console.log('timeout inside promise'),0); }) .then(() => console.log('promise 2')); console.log('end'); 

start
end
promise 1
promise 2
timeout 1
promise inside timeout
timeout inside promise
answers.10:
function batchUpdates(items) { items.forEach(item => { Promise.resolve().then(() => updateDOM(item)); }); }

Although the Promises are async they are still apart of Micro tasks which are flushed out before moving on. It would be better if we can wrap it around a debounce function which will ensure the batches are clubbed together when executed
